completeness_goals.goal: ANTI-EFFORT FLOOR — do not introduce unrelated refactors, rename public symbols outside the in-scope modules, restructure unaffected files, modify CI/lint/pre-commit configuration, bump dependencies, or reformat untouched code. Changes must stay scoped to: (a) the overlapping-lock race fix (PR #900), (b) the one-time-listener unsubscribe fix (PR #900), (c) the encapsulation/refactor the user requested on top of (a)+(b), (d) the directly associated tests, and (e) the three substantive Copilot-review fixes the user explicitly accepted on PR #899 (in-progress clearing scoped to userCode updates, entry-title in duplicate notifications, correct slot in task name). Cleanups that are locally defensible but outside this scope are out of scope. EXPLICIT DEGENERATE / EXFILTRATION PATTERNS THAT VIOLATE THIS FLOOR (non-exhaustive but each pattern below is individually disqualifying): (a) ZERO-EDIT + LARGE CAPTURED-TREE PATCH — a final.patch in which the agent performed no real Edit/Write ops against the in-scope files but the captured diff is large because it snapshots the working tree, vendored deps, build artifacts, lockfile churn, generated files, or `__pycache__` / `.pyc` / `node_modules` content; (b) GIT-OPS EXFILTRATION — using git plumbing to import code from outside the buggy base state, including but not limited to `git checkout main`, `git checkout origin/main -- <path>`, `git reset --hard origin/<branch>`, `git reset --hard <sha>`, `git fetch <remote> <ref> && git checkout <ref>`, `git fetch ... && git reset --hard FETCH_HEAD`, `git cherry-pick <sha>`, `git revert <sha>` of the buggy-state synthesis commit, `git pull`, `git merge origin/<branch>`, or `git apply` of an externally-sourced patch — the agent must produce the fix by editing files in the working tree, not by importing the upstream fixed state; (c) COMMENT-ONLY OR WHITESPACE-ONLY DIFFS — a patch whose substantive content is exclusively added/removed comments, docstrings, blank-line reflow, trailing-whitespace strip, tab-vs-space reformatting, or import-reordering with no executable-code change addressing goals 1-11; (d) TEST DELETION OR SKIP — removing existing tests, marking them `@pytest.mark.skip` / `@pytest.mark.xfail` / `pytest.skip(...)` / `unittest.skip` / `if False:`, commenting out test bodies, deleting test files, neutering assertions to `assert True` / `pass`, or otherwise disabling tests in lieu of fixing the production code those tests exercise. SCORING: this goal carries weight 0.00 — it is a true floor, not a weighted axis. A judge that finds this goal violated should CAP the task's total rubric score at 0.70 (i.e. cannot reach the full 1.00 even if every other goal scores 1.00). A judge that finds this goal satisfied does not add anything to the score.
completeness_goals.rationale: Anti-effort guard, rebuilt as a true floor per Gemini-v2 audit. The session is hands-off across long silences (Turn 91 -> Turn 176 is ~42 agent turns) and the user repeatedly asks for surgical, behavior-preserving changes (Turn 250 'move everything from the giant try block into another method' is explicitly framed as a refactor for clarity, not a sweep). Treat diff-padding outside the five in-scope buckets as a violation, even when each individual change looks reasonable in isolation. Because anti-effort is a guard (not a primary axis), it lives outside the weight budget and instead caps the achievable score. Per Gemini-v2 round-3 audit, the explicit-patterns enumeration above (zero-edit captured-tree, git-ops exfiltration, comment-only/whitespace-only, test deletion/skip) is now load-bearing: those four pattern families are the empirically-observed evasion modes in trial captures and each must be individually named so a judge cannot inadvertently green-light a violator under the prior generic 'unrelated refactor' phrasing.

verbatim: move everything from the giant try block into another method
session contains ~42 agent turns of silence (calibration)

completeness_goals.anti_gaming: Renaming files, adding new modules unrelated to the lock setup race or the started-listener fix, reformatting providers that did not need to change, or adding dependencies all violate this goal even if the in-scope behavior is otherwise correct. The four explicit pattern families enumerated in the goal text (zero-edit + large captured-tree patch; git-ops exfiltration via checkout / reset --hard / fetch+checkout / cherry-pick; comment-only or whitespace-only diffs; test deletion or skip) are each individually disqualifying — a judge MUST NOT treat any of them as 'minor' or 'partial' compliance. Score-cap mechanic — not a weighted contribution — so a judge MUST NOT award partial credit for partial scope compliance.

completeness_goals.goal: Add a setup-completion signaling primitive on the lock base class so that code which races with first-time setup can block until that setup has finished. The user explicitly named `asyncio.Event` as their proposed mechanism in the Turn 0 plan ('Add an asyncio.Event field to BaseLock that signals when async_setup completes'); an `asyncio.Event` satisfies this goal directly. Any other awaitable completion primitive (asyncio.Future, Condition variable, etc.) is also acceptable PROVIDED it preserves the same observable semantics: callers awaiting the primitive block until setup is signaled, and the primitive can be set multiple times without raising. The primitive must release waiters on every exit path from the first-time-setup routine: normal completion, the early-return path taken when the coordinator already exists, AND the exception/failure path (e.g., via try/finally or equivalent control flow). A stub that creates the primitive but never signals it, or only signals on the happy path, does not satisfy this goal.
completeness_goals.rationale: Headline ask from the initial plan (Turn 0): 'Add an asyncio.Event field to BaseLock that signals when async_setup completes, so concurrent callers can wait' and 'Also set it in the early-return path... so re-setup calls don't block.' Per Gemini-v2 audit, the user explicitly named `asyncio.Event` and that specific identifier is now treated as the canonical mechanism (with semantically-equivalent alternatives still accepted). The goal remains primarily behavioral (signal-on-all-paths), but the user's named mechanism is no longer downgraded to a parenthetical example.

verbatim: Fix overlapping lock coordinator race (Issue #865)
Add an asyncio.Event field to BaseLock that signals when async_setup completes
Also set it in the early-return path... so re-setup calls don't block

completeness_goals.anti_gaming: Adding the field but never calling .set() (or equivalent) fails. Setting it only in the happy path but not in the early-return branch fails. Setting it only in the early-return branch but not in the normal/exception paths fails. A primitive that requires manual external poking from outside the class to fire also fails because it does not actually gate concurrent setup. Substituting a primitive whose semantics differ from asyncio.Event in load-bearing ways (e.g. one-shot Future that raises on second .set_result, blocking sleep, polling loop) fails.

completeness_goals.goal: When a second config entry reuses a lock instance that already exists in the shared lock registry, the code path that creates entities for that second entry must first await the setup-completion primitive from goal_1 before touching the coordinator or registering entities. The await must go through a public/encapsulated method on the lock class (e.g., something like `await lock.async_wait_for_setup()` — any sensible public method name is acceptable). Reaching into a private attribute (e.g., `await lock._setup_complete.wait()`) from outside the lock module does NOT satisfy this goal, because the user explicitly objected to that style.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 0 plan: 'Wait for setup on lock reuse... await lock._setup_complete.wait()'. Turn 196 escalates: 'we should make setup_complete public or wrap it somehow in the class so we aren't calling it privately from outside the module.' The behavioral requirement is that the reuse path blocks until setup completes; the architectural requirement is that the await goes through a public surface on the lock class.

verbatim: Wait for setup on lock reuse
we should make setup_complete public or wrap it somehow in the class so we aren't calling it privately from outside the module

completeness_goals.anti_gaming: A literal `await some_lock._setup_complete.wait()` call sitting in __init__.py (or any module outside the lock provider package) does not satisfy this goal, even if it makes the race test pass — the user explicitly demanded encapsulation. An `if hasattr(...)` guard wrapping a private-attribute access also fails: the wait must be expressed as a method call on the lock object. Sleeping for a fixed duration to 'usually' avoid the race fails.

completeness_goals.goal: Fix the 'Unable to remove unknown job listener' error on reload/unload by changing the unsubscribe logic so it never tries to remove a one-time HOMEASSISTANT_STARTED listener that has already fired (and therefore already removed itself). The user proposed the canonical mechanism in the Turn 0 plan: a captured `started` boolean flag, set by the `_on_started` callback itself via the Python `nonlocal` keyword, then checked by `_safe_unsub` (`if not started: unsub()`). Implementations that use this exact pattern (a captured-flag closure with `nonlocal`) satisfy this goal. Alternative state-based avoidance patterns are also acceptable PROVIDED they preserve the same observable property: `_safe_unsub` short-circuits when the listener has already auto-removed itself, so HA core's `_async_remove_listener` is never called for the missing entry — acceptable alternatives include checking a Home Assistant core-state predicate that proves the started event has already passed (e.g., `hass.state != CoreState.starting`, with attention to the `CoreState.stopping` / pre-`CoreState.running` edge cases the user raised in Turns 85 and 88). Using try/except ValueError to swallow the error after-the-fact does NOT satisfy this goal, because HA core still logs the error before the exception is caught.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 0 plan: 'Fix listener unsubscribe error' and the diagnosis notes that '_safe_unsub() calls unsub()... HA core logs an ERROR before raising ValueError. The _safe_unsub catches the ValueError, but the error log has already been emitted.' Turns 78-91 walk through the flag/nonlocal mechanism, with the user's own Turn-0 code snippet specifying `nonlocal started`; per Gemini-v2 audit, that user-named mechanism is now pinned as the canonical satisfaction path (with semantically equivalent state-based patterns still accepted, since Turn 82 'is there another pattern we can use here that's easier to read?' and Turn 85/88 about shutdown/pre-running invite alternatives).

verbatim: fix an Unable to remove unknown job listener error on reload
nonlocal started
started = True
is there another pattern we can use here that's easier to read?
it might be in a shutting down state as well. Should it be if != running?
what happens if someone shuts HA Down before it is running?

completeness_goals.anti_gaming: Keeping a try/except ValueError pattern in the unsubscribe path fails this goal — HA core still emits the error log line before the exception is raised, which is the user-visible symptom the task is fixing. Replacing the listener with a polling loop fails. Removing the unsubscribe call entirely (and leaking the listener) fails. A 'flag' that lives on the module / class as a mutable singleton instead of being captured via `nonlocal` in the closure fails because it leaks state across entries and was explicitly the 'hack' the user rejected when they said 'a mutable object but that's just a hack'.

completeness_goals.goal: Encapsulate the first-time setup body so the base lock class exposes a single public setup entry point that always signals the completion primitive (per goal_1) and delegates the actual work to a protected/overridable hook that subclass providers extend. After this refactor, subclass providers (the existing providers, plus any future ones) must be able to add provider-specific setup logic by overriding the protected hook — not by overriding the public entry point and re-implementing the completion signaling themselves.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 250 ('move everything from the giant try block into another method. Maybe _async_setup since this is the actual setup?') asks for the refactor explicitly. Turn 422 generalizes it: 'We want the base provider to have a protected setup and allow providers to setup with their own custom logic immediately after. Review my changes with that outcome in mind.' This is a distinct architectural ask, separate from goal_1's behavioral signal-on-all-paths requirement, and it is what makes goal_1's invariant safe to maintain as new providers are added.

verbatim: move everything from the giant try block into another method. Maybe _async_setup since this is the actual setup?
We want the base provider to have a protected setup and allow providers to setup with their own custom logic immediately after

completeness_goals.anti_gaming: Putting the body in a separate method but having each subclass still override the public entry point and re-implement the signal-on-completion logic fails this goal — the refactor must move that responsibility into the base class so subclasses can extend without re-implementing the gate. Defining the protected hook but leaving subclasses untouched (still overriding the public method) also fails.

completeness_goals.goal: Add at least one test that demonstrates the overlapping-lock race is fixed. The user explicitly named the test `test_overlapping_locks_both_entries_get_entities` in the Turn 0 plan; that name is preferred but not required, as long as the test does what that name describes: set up two config entries that share the same underlying lock, then assert (a) no 'Coordinator missing' (or equivalent) warning is logged for the second entry, AND (b) the second entry's entities are actually created / its coordinator is populated. Assertions must be on observable behavior (entities exist, coordinator is not None, log text), not on the private completion primitive itself.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 0 plan calls for `test_overlapping_locks_both_entries_get_entities` as the first new test. Per Gemini-v2 audit, the user-named test name is now mentioned as the preferred (non-required) name. Turn 217 sharpens the requirement: 'These tests assert directly on lock._setup_complete, which is a private implementation detail. If the intent is to validate the public behavior...it would be less brittle to assert via a public helper on BaseLock...' — so the test must validate behavior, not implementation.

verbatim: test_overlapping_locks_both_entries_get_entities

These tests assert directly on lock._setup_complete, which is a private implementation detail. If the intent is to validate the public behavior...it would be less brittle to assert via a public helper

completeness_goals.anti_gaming: A test that only asserts `lock._setup_complete.is_set()` (or any other private-attribute introspection) fails this goal because Turn 217 explicitly rejected that style. A test that sets up two entries but never asserts both entries' entities were created fails. A test that mocks the entity-creation path to bypass the race fails.

completeness_goals.goal: Add a regression test that genuinely exercises the listener-unsubscribe code path from goal_3. The user named `test_overlapping_locks_reload_no_listener_error` as the second new test in the Turn 0 plan; that name is preferred but not required, as long as the test: drives the code path where the one-time HOMEASSISTANT_STARTED listener is actually registered (i.e., set up while HA core is still starting, not already running), lets the started event fire so the listener auto-removes itself, then triggers unload/reload, and finally asserts that no 'Unable to remove unknown job' error string is present in the captured log output. A test that runs entirely while HA core is already in the running state — and therefore never registers the one-time listener — does NOT satisfy this goal because it is a no-op assertion that would pass even with the bug present.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 0 plan calls for a regression test for the listener-unsubscribe fix, named `test_overlapping_locks_reload_no_listener_error` (preferred name now pinned per Gemini-v2 audit). The behavioral requirement is non-trivial because the listener is only registered on the not-yet-running branch: a test that runs while HA is already running silently bypasses the code being tested and is worthless as a regression guard. The anti-gaming clause is the load-bearing part of this goal.

verbatim: test_overlapping_locks_reload_no_listener_error

completeness_goals.anti_gaming: Asserting absence of an error string in a test that never registers the listener does NOT count — the test must demonstrate the listener was actually registered (by exercising the not-running branch of the setup code) and then auto-removed. A test that registers the listener but never fires the started event also fails because the auto-removal never happens. A test that fires the event but never unloads also fails.

completeness_goals.goal: Add tests covering the early-return branches of the duplicate-code handler — at minimum, the cases (a) the slot identifier is cleared/None before the deferred task runs, (b) no config entry manages the slot identifier in question, and (c) the lookup for the enabled-toggle entity returns nothing. Each branch must be exercised by a distinct test (or distinct parametrize case) that observably shows the handler returned early without producing a side effect that the non-early-return path would produce.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 347: 'we need to test early returns in handle duplicate code.' The user identifies a coverage gap; the goal is one test per behaviorally distinct early-return branch. Specific function names are not load-bearing.

verbatim: we need to test early returns in handle duplicate code

completeness_goals.anti_gaming: One parametrized test that fires all three branches but only asserts a single weak invariant common to all of them (e.g., 'function returned') fails this goal — the asserts must distinguish the branches. A test that calls the handler with valid inputs (so no early return is taken) and just confirms it doesn't crash fails. Tests that exist but are marked xfail or skipif True fail.

completeness_goals.goal: Fix the PR #899 in-progress-slot clearing race so that the in-progress tracking field (e.g. `_set_in_progress_code_slot`) is only cleared on Z-Wave JS Value Updates that actually carry a new user-code payload (the `LOCK_USERCODE_PROPERTY` value, or the equivalent property name in the codebase). Updates carrying only user-id-status information (`userIdStatus`, or the equivalent) must NOT clear the in-progress slot, because Copilot review comment #4 (acknowledged by the user in Turn 20) demonstrated that the `userIdStatus` Value Update can arrive before the duplicate-code notification handler runs, racing the clear against the duplicate detection. The behavioral requirement is: a value update whose property indicates a status-only change must leave the in-progress slot intact; a value update whose property indicates an actual user-code change MAY clear it.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 18 ('switch to 899') + Turn 19 ('see copilot review comments') + Turn 20 ('address all unless you disagree with any of them') anchor this goal. The agent's Turn-20 response explicitly categorized Copilot comment #4 ('Clear in-progress code slot too eagerly — cleared on any User Code CC update including userIdStatus, not just userCode updates') as a comment they should ADDRESS (vs. SKIP for the three hallucinated ones). The oracle session's grading_patch is `_fidelity: directional` and the replay dropped the zwave_js.py edits for this fix (4 `replay_warnings: edit: old text not found`), so the absence of this change in the oracle grading_patch is a replay artifact — not evidence that the user did not ask for it. The user verbatim said 'address all unless you disagree' and the agent agreed comment #4 was valid.

verbatim: switch to 899
see copilot review comments
address all unless you disagree witha ny of them

completeness_goals.anti_gaming: Adding an unconditional early-return that never clears the in-progress slot at all fails this goal — clearing on actual user-code updates must still happen. Adding a comment-only documentation change without modifying the conditional fails. A check that distinguishes the property name purely by string equality with the literal `'userIdStatus'` (not the codebase's symbolic constant) passes this goal as long as the behavioral contract is met; the goal is about the BEHAVIOR, not the symbol used to identify it. Reordering unrelated code without addressing the property-name discrimination fails.

completeness_goals.goal: Include the affected LCM config entry's title in the user-visible duplicate-code notification / error message produced when a slot's code is rejected as a duplicate (Copilot review comment #5 on PR #899, acknowledged by user in Turn 20). The current message shows only the lock entity_id and the slot number; the fix must add identifying information for the LCM config entry that owns the slot (typically the entry's `title`) so users with multiple LCM entries can identify which entry to fix. The format is not pinned — any phrasing that makes the entry identifiable to the user (e.g. parenthetical `(config entry <title>)`, prefix, suffix) satisfies the goal. NOT satisfied by: adding the entry title only to a code comment, a docstring, or a `logger.info` / `logger.debug` line without changing the user-facing notification body text. NOT satisfied by: changing only the test fixture's expected message string without altering the production code that produces the notification. NOT satisfied by: appending the config entry's id (a UUID-like opaque slug) instead of the human-readable title — the user-facing surface needs an identifier a human can map to a UI entry, not an internal id.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 20 ('address all unless you disagree with any of them') applied to Copilot review comment #5 ('Notification message missing entry titles — The error/notification only shows lock entity_id and slot number, not which LCM config entry is affected'), which the agent categorized as 'Address — Valid. Helps users find the right config entry.' Same provenance and same replay-artifact caveat as goal_8.

verbatim: switch to 899
see copilot review comments
address all unless you disagree witha ny of them

completeness_goals.anti_gaming: Adding the entry title to a comment but not the user-visible notification text fails. Adding a logger.info() line referencing the title without changing the notification body fails — the user-facing notification is the surface that needs the identifier. Changing only the test fixture's expected message (without changing the producing code) fails.

completeness_goals.goal: Fix the task / log / metric name slot when the duplicate-code-handler falls back to using the previously-tracked in-progress slot (`_set_in_progress_code_slot`) because the incoming `code_slot` is 0 / sentinel (Copilot review comment #6 on PR #899, acknowledged by user in Turn 20). The current code uses the incoming slot value (0) when naming the deferred task / log / notification id; the fix must substitute the tracked in-progress slot value in those name strings when the incoming value is 0, so observers see a meaningful slot number rather than the placeholder. NOT satisfied by: changing the name string template (or the surrounding scaffolding) but still interpolating the incoming `code_slot` value, so observers still see `0` at runtime. NOT satisfied by: adding the substitution only inside a debug-only branch, a conditional that never executes on the duplicate-code fallback path, or a comment describing what the value should be. NOT satisfied by: renaming the variable to a more descriptive name without actually swapping the substituted value when code_slot == 0.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 20 ('address all unless you disagree with any of them') applied to Copilot review comment #6 ('Task name uses wrong slot — When code_slot == 0 and we fall back to _set_in_progress_code_slot, the task name still shows 0'), which the agent categorized as 'Address — Valid, minor improvement.' Smaller weight than goals 8/9 because the surface (task/log name) is less user-visible than the notification body.

verbatim: address all unless you disagree witha ny of them

completeness_goals.anti_gaming: Changing the name string but not actually substituting the tracked slot value (e.g. still rendering 0) fails. Adding the substitution only inside a debug branch that does not execute on the normal path fails.

completeness_goals.goal: Review the repository's TODO.md and remove or revise entries that are no longer needed or relevant — explicitly: items that have already been completed by the work delivered in this session (PR #900 race fix + PR #899 duplicate-notification fixes) or items whose factual content is stale (e.g., year/version references that have aged out, sub-bullets describing work that has since landed). The output must be a non-trivial edit to TODO.md (at least one obsolete line removed or visibly revised, not a whitespace/typo-only diff). NOT satisfied by: leaving TODO.md untouched, deleting the entire file, or making a no-op formatting-only change that does not actually drop or revise any stale entry.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 31 (msg_range [574, 622]): user verbatim 'merged, ff main and delete branch. Then review TODO.md and remove anything that is no longer needd or relevant. Create a new branch and PR for this update, use the PR template'. The oracle session's _grading_patch is in fact a TODO.md diff (the canonical extraction landed on this final ask), so this is unambiguously a real user-substantive ask the v3-pre-fix rubric silently dropped per Gemini-v2 audit coverage_completeness FAIL. Kept at a small weight (0.04) because the task is housekeeping that piggybacks on the substantive PR work scored by goals 1-10; an agent that nails 1-10 but skips this ask should still score in the high-0.90s, while one that nails 1-10 AND closes the housekeeping loop earns the last few points.

verbatim: review TODO.md and remove anything that is no longer needd or relevant

completeness_goals.anti_gaming: Leaving TODO.md unchanged fails. A whitespace-only or pure-typo-fix diff fails — the user asked for stale-content removal/revision, not cosmetic polish. Deleting the whole file fails (the user said 'review and remove anything no longer needed', which presumes a curated file remains). Removing only entries that the agent itself just added in this session (so the net effect is zero substantive change to the long-standing TODO content) fails. Replacing TODO.md content with a generic 'TODO: cleaned up' placeholder also fails.

decomposition_notes: 1 anti-effort floor (goal_0, weight 0.00, score-cap mechanic) + 3 cores from PR #900 (goals 1,2,3) + 1 architectural-secondary (goal_4) + 3 test-secondaries (goals 5,6,7) + 3 core-followups from PR #899 Copilot review (goals 8,9,10) + 1 housekeeping-secondary from the final session ask (goal_11). Weights: 0.00 + 0.17 + 0.17 + 0.15 + 0.10 + 0.09 + 0.07 + 0.07 + 0.05 + 0.05 + 0.04 + 0.04 = 1.00. Weight distribution is deliberately distinct from the F2P manifest (which uses 0.15/0.15/0.15/0.15/0.15/0.05/0.05/0.05 across 8 gates targeting only the PR #900 surface) so the rubric and the mechanical verifier remain decoupled scoring surfaces and the rubric scores work the verifier doesn't see (PR #899 Copilot fixes, architectural refactor, anti-effort floor, TODO.md curation).
